narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.70, an LSAT score of 34, and details about your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, along with your part-time enrollment plans and lower-middle family income background. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 51% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average; this stood out as the strongest signal to the model that your later academic performance might not translate to bar success. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.70—higher than the average of 3.21—strongly pulled the prediction in the favorable direction, suggesting your college record showed real promise. Your LSAT score of 34, while below the dataset average of 36.70, also nudged things positively, indicating it helped rather than hurt your case. These three elements—your Year 3 deciles, undergrad GPA, and LSAT—were by far the biggest drivers in the model's decision, outweighing other details like your family income or part-time status. Compared to typical students, who often rank higher in later-year deciles and score stronger on the LSAT, your profile showed some strengths but was held back most by that dip in Year 3 performance. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially as a first-generation or underrepresented applicant navigating these metrics. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, and focus on strategies to improve your later-year grades, perhaps through tutoring or study groups tailored to law school prep. You might also highlight any extenuating circumstances around your Year 3 deciles in a revised application or appeal, emphasizing your solid undergrad foundation. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas—your higher-than-average GPA is a great starting point to build from. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
